segments.s233-g113: بسی is an adverbial intensifier ('much/very') placed before the noun آفرین ('praise, acclamation'). کرد is the past-tense third-person singular of کردن and is the main verb 'did/made', so آفرین کرد together functions as the verbal phrase 'praised'. بر introduces the target of the praise (a prepositional phrase); آفرین کردن بر X is a fixed collocation 'to praise X'. The order is adverb + object/noun + verb + prepositional target, typical of older Persian/poetic style.
segments.s233-g114: که here introduces a subordinate clause (a reported wish or benediction). او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. باد is not the noun 'wind' but the optative or jussive particle 'may (he) be' used in classical/poetic Persian to express a wish. شادان و روشن‌روان are predicate adjectives joined by و ('and'), giving the desired qualities: 'joyful and bright‑souled.' In sum, the clause reads 'that he may be joyful and bright‑souled.'
segments.s233-g9: A list of direct objects comes before the verb in Persian literary order: سپهر و زمان و زمین (the heavens, time, and the earth). آفرید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular literary verb meaning 'created' (the implicit subject is the creator/God). The conjunction و simply links the items in the series.
segments.s233-g10: Two coordinated object phrases (روان و خرد) are followed by داد, a past 3rd-singular verb 'gave' (so 'he gave soul and wisdom'), then و دین آفرید resumes the parallel clause with a fresh verb آفرید 'created religion'. This alternating pattern (object(s) + verb) is normal in Ferdowsi's diction; روان here = 'soul/spirit', خرد = 'wisdom'.
segments.s233-g11: آفرین باد is an optative formula: آفرین 'praise' and باد is the optative particle 'may there be'. بر شهریار = 'upon the king' (بر + noun). وزو is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'از او' ('from/of him' or 'to him') that introduces the beneficiary; it functions like a prepositional phrase placed before the wish.
segments.s233-g12: مبادا is a negative optative ('may it not be'); زمانه here = 'the age/era' (subject/topic). ازو = 'of/from him' (object of being a memorial), and یادگار = 'memorial, memento'. The phrase thus literally says 'May it not be that the age is a memorial of him' (i.e. 'may time leave no memorial of him').
segments.s233-g13: رسیدم is 1st-person past 'I arrived/reached'. بفرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by order/of the command' (ب + فرمان), and میان دو کوه = 'between two mountains' specifies the place. Word order places the place/adjunct after the governing verb in this line for metrical/literary effect.
segments.s233-g14: سپاه دو کشور is a genitival construction 'the army of two countries' (i.e. forces of both realms). شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'has become' or 'were become' and همگروه ('same-group' / 'allied') is a predicate adjective: 'the armies of the two realms had become allied.' Word order (noun phrase + شده + predicate) is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s233-g15: همانا که is an emphatic opener 'truly/indeed that'. شمشیرزن 'swordsman' is the noun being emphasized; the following numeral phrase (صد هزار) quantifies or intensifies him (classical Persian often places numerals after the noun for emphasis). The clause frames the subject about to be compared.
segments.s233-g16: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against' (here 'compared to'), دشمن = 'enemy', فزون 'more/in excess' functions as predicate adjective, and بود is past 'was'. در کارزار 'in the battle' is the locative adjunct. Together: 'he was more than the enemy in the battle' — the numerical phrase earlier supplies 'a hundred thousand' as the amount of excess.
segments.s233-g105: The personal pronoun تو (you) is fronted for emphasis; از + noun (ازِ درد و سختی) marks the source/what they were rescued from. رهانیدیم is a past-tense, first-person-plural verb (archaic/epic -یدیم ending) meaning “we freed/rescued,” so the clause literally reads “We freed you from pain and hardship.”
segments.s233-g106: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in epic Persian (here meaning ‘likewise/indeed’). تاج را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker. پرورانیدیم is past first-person-plural ‘we nurtured / we fostered,’ so the clause says “we nurtured the crown.”
segments.s233-g107: زمین و زمان is a compound subject (“earth and time”). پیش من is a prepositional phrase meaning “in my presence / before me,” and بنده شد is a predicative construction with شد (past ‘became’) giving “became servants.” The verb is past and the predicate follows the prepositional phrase.
segments.s233-g108: جهانی here means ‘the whole/world’ and is the subject. ز (poetic form of از) marks source ‘from,’ so ز گنج من = ‘from my treasure.’ آگنده is an adjective ‘filled/overflowing’ used predicatively with شد (‘became/was’), yielding “the world became filled from my treasure.”
segments.s233-g109: سپاس is the noun ‘thanks’; از تو دارم literally ‘I have (my) thanks from you,’ i.e. ‘I thank you.’ نه از انجمن negates the other possible source: ‘not from the assembly.’ دارم is present-tense ‘I have’ used idiomatically to express giving thanks.
segments.s233-g110: این یکی is elliptically used as ‘one / alone’ (یکی), followed by the appositive جان رستم ‘Rostam’s life/soul’ identifying the one addressed (تو). The copula ‘is’ is omitted; مستان ز من is a predicative phrase ‘intoxicated/ecstatic because of me’ (مستان used poetically as predicate), so the sense is ‘you, Rostam’s life, are intoxicated by me.’
segments.s233-g111: بزد is past third-person singular ‘he struck/smote’ with the object اسپ (steed) placed after it (poetic ordering). و is the conjunction ‘and.’ زان is the fused prepositional demonstrative ‘from that,’ جایگه is ‘place,’ and بازگشت here functions as the past verb ‘he returned.’
segments.s233-g112: بران is an adverbial ‘thereupon / upon that.’ پیل وان is ‘that elephant’ (پیل elephant + وان demonstrative ‘that’). بستگان (‘relatives/companions’) is a plural noun, and برگذشت is past ‘passed by / went past,’ so the clause says those creatures passed by thereafter.
segments.s233-g17: A simple coordinated noun list of peoples/peoples' names linked by the conjunction و (and). Each item is presented in nominal form; the conjunction و connects them in the usual head-to-tail order of Persian lists. شگنی is archaic/obscure here and the whole phrase functions as the topical/left-hand element that the following noun phrase (سپاهی ...) specifies further.
segments.s233-g18: A noun phrase giving the extent/source of the army: سپاهی (a host/army) followed by the prepositional phrase ز چین 'from China' and تا ... 'as far as' marking the limit. بدریای is a poetic or contracted form meaning 'to the sea' (here taking سند as the sea's name), so the sequence means 'an army from China as far as the Sindh Sea.'
segments.s233-g19: An extent phrase introduced by ز ... تا 'from ... to ...' indicating geographical range: ز کشمیر 'from Kashmir' تا دامن رود شهد 'as far as the hem/skirt (i.e. banks/foothills) of the river of honey.' Note the genitive relationship (دامنِ رودِ شهد) is typical in poetry where the linking ezāfe may be omitted.
segments.s233-g20: A sequence of direct objects followed by the verb دیدیم 'we saw': 'سراپرده و پیل' are coordinated objects (inner chambers and elephants), دیدیم is the past first-person plural verb, and the final و مهد adds another object (and a cradle/birthplace). Persian places the verb after its objects in such narrative lines.
segments.s233-g21: نترسیدم is the negative past first-person singular 'I did not fear.' The preposition از introduces the object/cause of fear, here دولت شهریار 'the sovereign's power/fortune' (دولت used in the sense of rule/sovereignty). The phrase as a whole states what the speaker did not fear.
segments.s233-g22: A subordinate clause introduced by کزین (from this/that of this) giving the feared eventuality: 'کزین رزمگاه اندر آید نهار' literally 'that from this battlefield day comes/should come.' اندر آید uses the present/future form of آمدن (آید) after اندر 'into/in', yielding 'come in' or 'appear'; نهار ('day') is used literally (daytime) and here names the event expected to arise from the battlefield.
segments.s233-g23: A temporal-duration clause where چهل روز 'forty days' marks the duration, followed by the circumstance با هم 'together' and the adverb همی 'continuously/still', with the copula/verb جنگ بود 'there was war' (literally 'war was'). This is the typical Persian order: temporal phrase + circumstantial phrases + verb.
segments.s233-g24: A reported-speech clause: تو گفتی 'you said' followed by the content بریشان جهان تنگ بود 'the world was narrow for them' (بریشان = 'for them/for those people', جهان تنگ بود = 'the world was narrow' i.e. too small). Note that گفتی is past second-person 'you said' and introduces the speaker's remark.
segments.s233-g65: چنین (thus) is a sentence‑initial adverb modifying the speech; گفت is the past 3rd‑person verb 'said' (the speaker is implied from context). با introduces the indirect object (the persons being addressed) and is followed by a coordinated list of proper names (طوس و گودرز و گیو). Word order is adverb → verb → prepositional phrase (with names).
segments.s233-g66: The clause begins with a vocative/introductory particle (که‌ای is a poetic vocative ‘O…’) addressing the addressees. نامداران is a plural substantive ‘renowned ones’; گردان نیو is a noun phrase 'band/formation (گردان) of the ney (نیو)', a poetic epithet for a group. The phrase functions as direct address to the listed men.
segments.s233-g67: من (I) is the explicit subject; این بار (this time) is a temporal phrase. چنگ (the harp / figurative instrument) is the direct object; اندر is an archaic locative 'in/into'; آرم is a 1st‑person form expressing the speaker's action (poetic/old form of 'I put/take'); بچنگ is a compound adverbial 'into (my) grasp' that links this action to the result clause that follows. The segment presents the agent, time, and the action of seizing/taking up an instrument.
segments.s233-g68: بداندیشگان را — بداندیشگان ('evil‑minded ones' / 'malefactors') is marked by the object/topic marker را as those affected; شود is 3rd‑person present/future of شدن 'become' (here 'will become'); کار تنگ is a predicate nominal ('affairs narrow' ≐ 'be hard‑pressed'). The clause expresses the expected result: the ill‑wishers will find themselves in difficulty.
segments.s233-g69: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('who/that'); دانست is past 3rd‑person 'knew' (here rhetorical: 'who would have known'); کین is a classical/poetic demonstrative modifier ('this' / emphatic) modifying the following noun phrase; چاره‌گر مرد سند is a noun phrase 'the problem‑solver / remedial man of Sind' where چاره‌گر qualifies مرد and سند is a regional attribution. Word order: relative particle → verb → emphatic demonstrative → modifier + head noun + regional qualifier.
segments.s233-g70: سپاه آرد is the verbal phrase 'brings an army' (آرد = poetic/older 3rd‑person of آوردن 'bring'); از is the preposition 'from' and is followed by a coordinated list of place names (چین، سقلاب و هند). The order is subject → verb → prepositional phrase listing origins.
segments.s233-g71: من (I) is subject; او را marks 'him' as the direct/affected object. چنان مست و بیهش is a coordinated predicative phrase 'so drunk and stunned/insensible' (بیهش is a poetic/archaic term meaning stunned/insensible or similar). کنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive used to state the speaker's intended action ('I will make'). The whole segment expresses intention to render the person intoxicated and senseless.
segments.s233-g72: تنش is تن + possessive suffix (his body); خاک گور سیاوش is a genitive string 'the earth/soil of Siavash's grave' (خاک = earth; گور = grave; سیاوش = proper name); کنم repeats the 1st‑person verb 'I will make'. The clause states the consequence: the speaker will make his body into the earth of Siavash's grave (i.e., cause his death and burial related to Siavash).
segments.s233-g57: همانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment' and sets the time. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; it introduces the source phrase دهلیز پرده‌سرای, a genitive/attributive sequence meaning 'the vestibule of the curtained chamber' (noun + modifier), so the whole phrase means 'from the vestibule of the curtained chamber.'
segments.s233-g58: برآمد is a 3rd-person past tense verb 'arose/issued forth.' It is followed by the verbal noun/infinitive phrase خروشیدن together with کرنای in a possessive/attributive relation, so the structure means 'the roaring (خروشیدن) of the trumpet (کرنای) arose' — a poetic order where the verb comes before the noun phrase denoting the sound.
segments.s233-g59: تهمتن is an epithet/name used as the subject (here referring to the hero). میان functions adverbially 'in the midst/middle (of)', taking the verbal noun تاختن 'the charge/gallop' as its complement; را is the definite object marker highlighting that action, and ببست is the past 3rd‑person 'he stopped/checked' (here 'he reined in the charge').
segments.s233-g60: بران باره is an adverbial phrase 'on that occasion / upon that instance' (بران = 'upon that', باره = 'occasion'). تیزتگ appears as a noun (likely a proper name here, e.g. of a mount or gear) serving as the object, and برنشست is the past 3rd‑person 'he sat upon/mounted.' Note the poetic omission of the object marker را.
segments.s233-g61: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said.' تا here introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('that/so that'), and توشه برداشتند is the embedded clause 'they took provisions' with برداشتند a past plural verb 'they took.' The sequence means 'he ordered that they take provisions.'
segments.s233-g62: همی is an adverb indicating immediate or continuing action (poetic). راه دشوار is the noun phrase 'the difficult road,' and بگذاشتند is a past plural verb meaning here 'they set out / departed / proceeded' (poetic usage). Together: 'they then proceeded on the difficult road.'
segments.s233-g63: بیابان گرفتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: گرفتند (past plural) is 'they took' used transitively with بیابان 'the wilderness'—i.e. 'they took to the desert.' و simply links the next phrase, and راه دراز is the noun phrase 'the long road' (object/goal).
segments.s233-g64: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (3rd singular). چنان 'thus/so' modifies the clause, and لشکری رزمساز is the subject phrase placed after the verb (poetic inversion) meaning 'an army fit for battle' or 'a war‑making army' — i.e. 'thus came an army ready for battle.'
segments.s233-g97: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'looked/gazed' (نگه = 'gaze', کرد = past 'did/made' used as the light/past auxiliary). The subject خسرو follows the verb in this poetic inversion (verb-subject order). بران = بر + آن 'at/those' attaches to بستگان, so the whole chunk means 'Khosrow looked at those بستگان.'
segments.s233-g98: This is a coordinated list: هیونان (plural form) = 'wild beasts/animals', و = 'and', پیلان = 'elephants', and آن خستگان = 'those weary ones' (آن is the demonstrative 'those/the'). The nouns are simply enumerated; the plural suffix -ان is a poetic plural.
segments.s233-g99: عنان را بپیچید is a transitive past action: عنان 'the reins' is marked by را (definite object marker) and بپیچید is past 'turned/drew in' (ب- prefix + پیچید). The clause ends with و آمد براه, where آمد ('came') with the adverbial براه 'onto the road/en route' means 'he set off' or 'came onto the road'.
segments.s233-g100: ز سر برگرفت is a short verbal clause: ز (poetic 'from'), سر 'head', and برگرفت 'took up/removed' (past). آن کیانی کلاه is the object: آن = 'that/the', کیانی 'Kayanian/royal' qualifying کلاه 'cap/hat' (here 'crown/cap'). Word order is verb after the prepositional phrase 'ز سر' so 'he took that Kayanian cap from (his) head.'
segments.s233-g101: فرود آمد is a past action meaning 'he descended/alighted' (فرود = descent/alighting + آمد = came). پیش یزدان = 'in the presence of God' (پیش = before/in the presence of), and بخاک indicates direction 'to the ground' (i.e. he cast himself down). The sequence shows motion + location: he came down and to God's presence fell to the ground.
segments.s233-g102: بغلتید is past tense meaning 'he rolled/turned over' (here describing rolling on the ground, part of prostration). و گفت links to the speech that follows: گفت is past 'he said'. ای جهاندار پاک is a vocative: ای = 'O', جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign', پاک an epithet 'pure'.
segments.s233-g103: ستمکاره‌ای (an oppressor) is the subject; کرد is the past verb 'did/committed'. The phrase بر من ستم uses بر + من to mark the target/indirect object ('against me') and ستم is the noun 'oppression/injustice' — literal structure 'An oppressor did injustice upon me.'
segments.s233-g104: مرا بی‌پدر کرد is a transitive causative clause: مرا (me, object; written here split into م + را) + بی‌پدر 'fatherless' + کرد 'made' = 'made me fatherless.' با درد و غم is an adverbial phrase 'with pain and sorrow' (با = with; درد = pain/suffering; غم = grief), describing the manner/effect.
segments.s233-g41: This phrase lists companions/items after a prepositional particle: the initial word (here written ابا) functions as the preposition 'with' in poetic diction (a prosthetic alef appears for meter). The coordinated nouns follow the pattern 'شاه و پیل و هیونی هزار' = 'the king and elephant(s) and a thousand beasts'.
segments.s233-g42: ازان رزمگه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' (ازان = 'from that', رزمگه = 'battlefield'). برنهادند is a past plural transitive verb 'they raised / they put up / they took up' (بر-نهاد-ند, poetic past). بار is the direct object meaning 'load/baggage' here and therefore 'they lifted/took up the baggage'. Word order is verb-final in the segment (prepositional phrase + verb + object in this poetic line).
segments.s233-g43: فریبرز کاوس appears as a name with apposition (Fereyburz, of Kavus); شادان is an adjective 'joyful' modifying the subject. برفت is a past-tense verb 'he went / set off' (poetic variant; here a simple past of رفتن). The order is Name + epithet + verb, typical of epic narration.
segments.s233-g44: بنزدیک خسرو is a locative phrase 'near Khosrow' (بنزدیک = 'close at hand / near'). بسیچید is a past tense transitive verb meaning 'he mustered / marshalled (troops)'. The coordinating و joins بسیچید to تفت; تفت here is a short verb-form whose literal cooking senses in the lexical list do not fit the context (so it functions poetically as 'advanced / moved on' or a similar motion verb).
segments.s233-g45: همی رفت is an iterative/continuous past 'he/they kept going' (همی gives a habitual/ongoing sense). با او = 'with him'. گو is a parenthetical particle 'they say' or 'so it is said' (here introducing reported motion). پیلتن is a place-name (or group name) in apposition to the following 'buzurgān' in the next segment.
segments.s233-g46: بزرگان و گردان آن انجمن lists who accompanied him: بزرگان 'the great ones / nobles', و 'and', گردان 'troop(s) / companies', آن انجمن 'of that assembly' (آن qualifies انجمن). The noun phrases are coordinated and function as the compound subject/object of the motion verb in the previous segment.
segments.s233-g47: به پدرود کردن is an infinitival phrase 'to make/perform a farewell' (به = 'for/to', پدرود = 'farewell', کردن = light verb 'to do / to say' used with پدرود). گرفتش is a past tense transitive 'he took/seized him' (گرفت + ـش = 'him'), and کنار 'aside/beside' indicates the location 'to one side' — together: 'he took him aside to bid farewell.'
segments.s233-g48: ببارید آب is a figurative clause 'water rained' (ببارید = 'it rained / poured down', آب = 'water' understood as tears). از غم شهریار is an ablative/causal phrase 'from the sorrow of the king' (از = 'because of / from', غم = 'sorrow', شهریار = 'the king'), so the whole clause means 'tears poured because of the king's grief.'
segments.s233-g33: همه شهریاران که دارند بند is a noun phrase with a relative clause: همه شهریاران (all the rulers/kings) modified by که دارند بند (who have a fetter). دارند is present (they have) and closes the relative clause; بند is the direct nominal object of دارند (fetter, bond). Word order is standard for Persian relative clauses: head (شهریاران) followed by که + verb phrase.
segments.s233-g34: ز پیلان گرفتم بخم کمند is a past-tense clause with the preposition ز (from) introducing پیلان (the elephants). گرفتم is 1st-person past 'I took/seized'. کمند is the direct object 'lasso/snare'; بخم is an archaic adjective/adverbial modifier of کمند (here left untranslated as a fixed descriptive element, 'coiled/curved' or otherwise archaic) and appears between verb and object in a poetic order. The whole segment supplies the action performed by the speaker (I took ... from the elephants).
segments.s233-g35: سوی جنگ دارم کنون رای و روی is built around دارم (I have). سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'toward war' (direction/purpose). کنون is the adverb 'now' modifying دارم. رای و روی is a coordinated object (ruling/counsel and face/resolve) that expresses what the speaker possesses: plan/counsel (رای) and 'روی' here used as personal resolve/face (poetic use).
segments.s233-g36: مگر پیش گرز من آید گروی begins with مگر 'unless' (a conditional/exceptive conjunction). پیش گرز من is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of my mace' (پیش + noun + possessive من). آید is the present/subjunctive-type form 'comes'. گروی is an infinitival/denominal element meaning 'to join/submit' (an older/poetic verbal noun); together آید گروی means 'comes to join / comes to submit'. The clause thus gives the exception to the previous statement.
segments.s233-g37: زبانها پر از آفرین تو باد is an optative/blessing. زبانها (tongues/speeches) is the subject, پر از آفرین 'full of praise' is a predicative phrase (پر + از + noun). تو is the possessor/target 'of you' and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical poetic mood forming a wish). Word order is subject + predication + optative.
segments.s233-g38: سر چرخ گردان زمین تو باد is a second benediction in which a genitive chain names what is wished to belong to the addressee: سر (summit/head) of چرخ گردان (the revolving wheel/sky) and زمین (the earth). تو is the possessor and باد again is the optative 'may it be yours'. Grammatically this is a compact appositive/genitive sequence ending in the optative verb.
segments.s233-g39: چو نامه بمهر اندر آمد بداد begins with چو 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نامه بمهر is a noun plus postpositive modifier: بمهر 'with a seal' (i.e., 'the sealed letter'). اندر آمد is the compound verbal phrase 'came in/was brought in' (اندر = in/into placed before آمد). بداد here functions as a proper name (agent/recipient in the narrative) rather than the common noun sense and so is best read as a person mentioned in the tale.
segments.s233-g40: بمهتر فریبرز خسرو نژاد is the following clause giving the addressee: read as به مهتر (to the chief) + personal name فریبرز (Fariborz) with the epithet/lineage خسرو نژاد (of Khosrow). مهتر here is a title 'chief/senior' and فریبرز is the proper name; خسرو نژاد is an attributive/epithet meaning 'born of/descended from Khosrow'. The fused spelling بمهتر is poetic/archaic spelling of به مهتر.
segments.s233-g73: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the clause; از is the preposition 'from' and begins a list of place-names. The sequence هند و سقلاب و توران و چین is simply a coordinated series of geographic names (joined by و 'and') functioning as the object of the following verb.
segments.s233-g74: نخوانند is a classical third-person plural form of خواندن here meaning 'they (shall) not recite/shall no longer recite' (the initial ن marks negation/jussive in older usage). ازین پس is the fixed temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). برو is an imperative 'go' and آفرین is an interjection 'Afarin' = 'bravo' (not the verb 'to create' here).
segments.s233-g75: بزد is the simple past 'he struck' with its object کوس 'the war-drum' following the verb (typical classical word order). وز here functions as a poetic preposition/particle (literally a blast/from) linking to دشت 'the plain' — together giving 'from the plain' or 'a blast from the plain'. برخاست is 'rose/was lifted' and گرد here is the noun 'dust' that rose.
segments.s233-g76: This is two parallel verbless clauses: 'هوا پر ز گرد' = 'the air (was) full of dust' and 'زمین پر ز مرد' = 'the ground (was) full of men'. پر is an adjective 'full (of)' followed by the poetic preposition ز 'of/from' and the noun that fills it; the coordinating و links the two halves.
segments.s233-g77: ازان is a compound prepositional/pronominal form 'from those / of those'. نامداران is a plural substantive 'the famed/renowned ones' and پرخاشجوی is an adjectival attribute 'quarrelsome / aggressive' describing them; the whole phrase identifies the group being referred to.
segments.s233-g78: بابر is a proper name (here the entrant). اندر آمد is the compound verb 'came in/entered' (اندر = in/among + آمد = came). یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)', and گفت و گوی is a nominalized phrase meaning 'a speech / a saying / a spoken word' (گفت as 'speech/conversation' and گوی 'saying'); together they indicate that someone spoke or began to speak.
segments.s233-g79: دو منزل is a numerical phrase 'two (manzils/stages)' where منزل here means a stage or stage-of-a-journey; برفتند is the third-person plural past 'they went/departed' and زان جایگاه 'from that place' gives the point of departure.
segments.s233-g80: The clause beginning with که gives the reason or result: که = 'that/for', از = 'from/because of', کشته is used as a noun 'the slain' (the corpses), بد‑روی is a descriptive compound literally 'ill‑faced / grim of face' modifying the effect on گیتی 'the world', and سیاه 'black/dark' completes the picture — i.e. the world was darkened by the slain.
segments.s233-g49: وزان is a poetic contraction meaning 'از آن' ('from that'); جایگه = 'place'. The clause uses simple past کشید (3rd person) meaning 'moved/drew' toward the following object. سوی governs the following noun (لشکر = 'army/host'), so the phrase reads 'from that place toward the army (he) moved.'
segments.s233-g50: چو here introduces a likeness ('like/as'); جعد دو زلف is a poetic noun phrase 'the curl of two locks' (جعد = curl, زلف = lock of hair) and is placed before the verb. آمد پدید is an inverted bicolon meaning 'came forth/appeared' (آمد = came, پدید = appeared/manifests). In sum: 'when/as the night appeared like two curled locks.'
segments.s233-g51: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat' (here the provided lemma was incorrect); با + noun-list ('آرامش و رود و می') marks accompaniment/instrumental circumstance: 'with calm and with the rūd and with wine.' رود in this context is the musical instrument (not a river).
segments.s233-g52: This is a balanced construction: 'یکی ... و دگر ...' = 'one ... and the other ...'. دَست = 'hand'; the verb is omitted and understood ('one hand (held) the rūd and the other hand (held) the ney'). نی here is the reed/ney (flute).
segments.s233-g53: برفتند is past plural 'they went/they departed.' هر کس = 'each person' (subject), and بآرام خویش is an adverbial phrase 'in his own quiet/calm manner' (بآرام = quietly; خوی + ش = 'his own disposition' → 'his own way').
segments.s233-g54: گرفته is a perfect participle 'having seized/ taken'; بَبر here functions as a verb indicating carrying off/taking away in classical usage (the lexicon senses given do not include this exact inflected/archaic form, hence ambiguity). The clause means 'each, having taken (it), carried off his own کام' — کام here = 'satisfaction/fulfillment'. Note the subject هر کسی ('each person') and possession expressed by کام خویش ('his own satisfaction').
segments.s233-g55: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause ('when'/'as'); خورشید = 'the sun'. با + رنگ دیبای زرد = 'with the color of yellow brocade' (دیبای = brocade/silk; زرد = yellow), an ablative/instrumental descriptive phrase modifying the sun's appearance.
segments.s233-g56: ستم کرد is a transitive verbal phrase 'did oppression' → 'oppressed/treated harshly'; بر introduces the target (توده لاژورد = 'the mass/heap of lapis-lazuli'—a poetic image for deep blue masses). Grammatically, ستم کرد governs بر + noun as its object; the clause personifies the sun as acting upon the blue masses.
segments.s233-g81: The clause contains two past actions. 'یکی' here means 'someone/one (person)' and is the subject of the second verb. 'بیشه دیدند' literally places the object 'بیشه' (grove) before the past plural verb 'دیدند' ('they saw'); the subject 'they' is omitted as usual. 'آمد فرود' is an inverted poetic order for the usual compound verb 'فرود آمد' (to alight/descend): 'آمد' (came) + 'فرود' (descent/alighting) — together 'one came down/aliked'.
segments.s233-g82: 'سیه شد' is the adjective 'سیه' (black/dark) followed by the past 'شد' (became), a common Persian structure meaning 'became black/dark'. The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') introduces the cause/agent: 'ز لشکر' = 'because of/from the host'. 'همه' qualifies 'دشت و رود' as 'the whole/entire plain and river' — i.e. the plain and the river alike were darkened by the army.
segments.s233-g83: 'همی' is an archaic/poetic imperfective particle meaning 'still/was (in that state)' and pairs with the past verb 'بود' to give a continuing past sense. 'با رامش' means 'with tranquility/repose'. The token 'می‌بدست' here should be read idiomatically as 'می + بدست' = 'wine in hand' (i.e. holding wine), not as the verb 'to obtain'; the supplied lemma is ambiguous but context shows a descriptive accompaniment.
segments.s233-g84: This balanced clause uses the repetitive 'یکی ... یکی ...' pattern meaning 'one ... another ...'. The copula is omitted (carried over from the previous 'بود'), so 'یکی شاد و خرم' = 'one (was) glad and joyful' and 'یکی خفته مست' = 'another (was) asleep and drunk/intoxicated'. Adjectives/participles follow the pattern common in Persian narrative.
segments.s233-g85: 'فرستاده' is 'messenger/envoy' and 'آمد' is the past 'came'. Poetic narrative sometimes uses singular forms generically; here it conveys that messengers arrived. 'ز' (poetic 'از') introduces source: 'ز هر کشوری' = 'from every country/land' (کشوری used substantively to mean 'land/country').
segments.s233-g86: This is a continuation enumerating origins: 'ز هر نامداری و هر مهتری' — 'from every namdar (the renowned/notable) and every mehtari (the great/chief)'. The repeated 'هر' ('every') emphasizes universality; 'ز' again marks origin/source.
segments.s233-g87: 'بسی' here functions as an intensifier meaning 'many/much' (archaic usage). It modifies the following nouns: 'هدیه' (gifts), 'ساز' (musical instruments/music), and 'چندی نثار' ('some offerings'). 'نثار' is presented nominally here ('offerings'), though its root is verbal ('to offer'); the list is a sequence of items presented.
segments.s233-g88: 'ببردند' is past plural 'they carried/brought'. 'نزدیک' here functions adverbially/locatively as 'to/near', so 'ببردند نزدیک آن نامدار' = 'they bore/ brought (them) to that famed one'. 'آن نامدار' = 'that renowned person'.
segments.s233-g89: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بگذشت is a past-tense verb meaning 'passed' (time passed); ازین داستان («از» + «ین» = 'from this') sets the temporal source 'since this tale'; روز چند ('a few days') is the time-phrase that bگذشت scopes over, placed after the noun in classical word order.
segments.s233-g90: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'; گردش here names the turning/revolution (the wheel's motion). بیاسود is used as a past-state 'came to rest / was at rest' (classical usage), so the clause says the wheel stopped turning; چرخ بلند is a poetic compound 'the lofty charkh' meaning the heavens/wheel of fortune.
segments.s233-g91: کس here is the indefinite 'someone' used idiomatically to introduce the arrival; آمد is past 'came'; بر + شاه ایران marks the person at whose presence they arrived ('before the King of Iran'); سپاه is the noun 'an army/host' identifying what came (classical word order allows the appositive placed after).
segments.s233-g92: که here introduces a clarifying relative/explanatory clause 'namely/that'; آمد is past 'came/arrived'; فریبرز کاوس شاه names the arriving person (Farībarz, Kay Kāvus) with شاه as an appositive/title.
segments.s233-g93: پذیره شدش is a compound passive/participial idea: پذیره (reception/welcome) + شدش (lit. 'it happened to him' / 'he was made to receive' with the enclitic ش = 'him'), so 'he was received' or 'he was welcomed.' شاه names the royal actor (the king) and کنداوران is an attached noun (here likely 'the attendants/retainers' in context), placed after شاه in classical order.
segments.s233-g94: This string lists the manner/ceremony of reception: (ابا) here functions in older/poetic Persian to link the instrumental phrase; بوق 'horn' and کوس 'war-drum' are joined by و 'and'; سپاهی گران 'heavy/weighty troops' describes the soldiery. The whole segment specifies that trumpets, drums and strong troops accompanied the reception.
segments.s233-g95: فریبرز is the subject (proper name). نزدیک خسرو ('near Khosrow') is an adverbial/prepositional phrase indicating movement toward Khosrow; رسید is the simple past 'arrived/approached.' Classical Persian often places the adverbial ('نزدیک') before the object name.
segments.s233-g96: زمین را ببوسید is a straightforward clause: زمین 'the ground' + را (definite object marker) + ببوسید (past 'he kissed it'). کو را بدید is a short follow-up clause where کو functions as an archaic/poetic relative/interrogative 'which/who' (here 'who it was') and را marks it as object of بدید ('he saw'), i.e. 'and he saw who it was.'
segments.s233-g1: دبیر جهاندیده را پیش خواند is a transitive clause: دبیر جهاندیده (the worldly‑wise official/scribe) is the definite object marked by را, and پیش خواند is the verb phrase ‘called/summoned before (him)’. پیش here functions adverbially ‘before/in the presence of’, and خواند is used in the courtly sense ‘summoned’ rather than ‘read’. The agent (‘he’) is omitted, as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s233-g2: سخن هرچ بایست با او براند is a subordinate result/permission clause: سخن هرچ (‘whatever speech/words’) is the theme, بایست is an impersonal/modal sense ‘ought/should be’, and با او marks the agent ‘by/with him’. براند (from راند) is used here in a permissive/causative sense ‘let [it] be put/uttered’ or ‘be delivered’ by him; the whole means ‘let him speak whatever needs to be said’. Word order places the theme first and the verb at the end, as in Persian.
segments.s233-g3: بفرمود تا introduces an ordered/requested subordinate clause: بفرمود is the honorific past ‘he commanded/said’, and تا here functions as a subordinating particle ‘that/so that’ introducing what was to be done. نامه خسروی is a noun phrase ‘the royal/kings’ letter’ with خسروی qualifying نامه.
segments.s233-g4: ز عنبر نوشتند بر پهلوی is the content of the order: ز عنبر is a prepositional phrase ‘of/from amber’ (poetic/material or fragrant ingredient), نوشتند is the narrative past plural ‘they wrote’, and بر پهلوی uses بر + language to mean ‘in Pahlavi/using the Pahlavi script’. The verb comes after its adverbial modifier and before the postpositional phrase, as is common in Persian.
segments.s233-g5: سرنامه کرد آفرین خدای is a clause in which سرنامه (‘prefatory heading/title’) is the object of کرد ‘made/did’, and آفرین خدای functions as the content of that heading — an exclamation ‘Praise God’ or praise-address to God. کرد is the simple past ‘he made/wrote (as)’, so the phrase means ‘he made as the heading: “Praise God”’.
segments.s233-g6: کجا هست و باشد همیشه بجای contains a short benediction: کجا هست ‘wherever He is’ (هست = present copula/existential), و باشد is an optative/subjunctive ‘and may He be/remain’, همیشه ‘always’, بجای ‘in place/in his place’. باشد is here jussive/optative rather than an imperative addressed to God, giving the sense ‘may He always remain’. The sequence is poetic and elliptical but grammatically parallels assertion and wish.
segments.s233-g7: برازنده ماه و کیوان و هور is a nominal predicate with an implied copula: برازنده ‘worthy/fitting’ takes the following list (ماه، کیوان، هور) — names/epithets of celestial bodies or radiant entities — as its logical arguments. The clause is stylistically compressed (the verb ‘is/are’ is omitted) as is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s233-g8: نگارنده فر و دیهیم و زور is a noun phrase in apposition/qualification: نگارنده ‘the writer/inscriber’ is followed by a coordinated list of what he inscribes or embodies — فر (farr, royal glory), دیهیم (rural/relating to the land or countryside), and زور (strength/might). Grammatically this completes the thought begun in the previous segment: these celestial things are ‘worthy of’ the writer of these qualities.
segments.s233-g25: همه شهریاران کشور بدند is a nominal subject phrase + verb. همه quantifies شهریاران کشور (‘the kings of the country/realm’ where کشور postmodifies شهریاران) and بدند is the poetic/plural form of ‘were’ (past copula). The segment therefore means 'all the kings of the realm were...'.
segments.s233-g26: نه negates the first manner ('not ...'); بر باد together is the idiom 'to be blown away / ruined' (here ‘not blown away’). The conjunction و functions adversatively ('but/and') to introduce the contrasting cause: با + بخت لاغر marks the instrument/cause ('by/with a thin/lean fate') and بدند repeats the copula 'they were'.
segments.s233-g27: میان دو کوه is a standard prepositional phrase 'between two mountains.' از بر is a classical prepositional combination meaning 'from the side of / over / along'; it links to the following coordinated nouns راغ و دشت ('rāgh and plain'), giving a locative span: 'between two mountains, over rāgh and plain.'
segments.s233-g28: ز خون و ز کشته are ablative/partitive phrases 'from/of blood and of the slain' describing what lies there; نشاید is an impersonal negative ('it is not fitting/possible'), and گذشت is the verbal notion 'to pass' (here rendered impersonal: 'it is not possible to pass'). Together: 'because of blood and slain, it is not possible to pass.'
segments.s233-g29: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/verily.' که introduces the clause or specification. فرسنگ (a measure) together with باشد (the copula in classical/subjunctive usage) and the numeral چهل yields a measure: 'it was (measured) forty parasangs.' The verb باشد here functions as the copula marking extent.
segments.s233-g30: This clause is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: پراگنده از خون ('scattered/strewn with blood') modifies the following nominal phrase; زمین is the subject, بود is the past copula 'was', and گل functions as a predicative noun ('a flower' — a metaphor). Literally: 'the earth was a flower, scattered with blood.'
segments.s233-g31: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'in conclusion.' از + ین together form 'ازین' ('of/from this') modifying دولت ('the state/sovereignty'), and دیریاز is an adjective 'long-standing/long-lived' describing that دولت. The phrase sets up the topic: 'finally, concerning this long-standing rule.'
segments.s233-g32: سخن گویم is 'I speak/tell a tale' with گویم as the first-person verb (classical subjunctive/present used for narration: 'I will tell'). این نامه گردد دراز is a result clause: این نامه ('this record/account') گردَد ('will become' / 'becomes') دراز ('long'). The overall sense: 'if I tell it, this account will grow long.' Note: the provided senses list for گویم does not contain the verbal sense 'to say' so its sense index is marked -1.

sentences.s233-s57: He praised the hero greatly, (saying) may he be joyful and bright‑souled.
sentences.s233-s5: He created the heavens and time and the earth; he gave (them) soul and wisdom, and he created religion.
sentences.s233-s6: Praise to him — may it be for the king of the age; may time leave no memorial of him.
sentences.s233-s7: I arrived, by command, between two mountains; the armies of the two realms had become united.
sentences.s233-s8: Truly — that swordsman was, in the battle, a hundred thousand more than the enemy.
sentences.s233-s53: We rescued you from pain and hardship, and we likewise nurtured the crown.
sentences.s233-s54: Earth and time became servants before me; the whole world was filled from my treasure.
sentences.s233-s55: I give thanks to you, not to the assembly; you alone — Rostam's very life — are intoxicated by me.
sentences.s233-s56: He struck his steed and from that place returned; thereupon that elephant and its companions passed by.
sentences.s233-s9: Kushans, Shagni, Chinese and Indians — a host from China as far as the Sindh Sea.
sentences.s233-s10: From Kashmir to the skirts (foothills) of the river of honey — we saw inner chambers and elephants and a cradle.
sentences.s233-s11: I was not afraid of the king's power, that from this battlefield day should come.
sentences.s233-s12: For forty days there was fighting together; you said the world was too narrow for them.
sentences.s233-s33: Thus he said to Tus and Goudarz and Giv: “O renowned ones and the company of the ney,”
sentences.s233-s34: This time I will take up the harp into my hand; if I get them in my grasp, the ill‑wishers will be hard‑pressed.
sentences.s233-s35: Who could have known that this remedial man of Sind would bring an army from China and Sogdiana and India?
sentences.s233-s36: I will make him so drunk and stupefied, and will make his body the earth of Siavash's grave.
sentences.s233-s29: At that moment a trumpet's roar issued forth from the vestibule of the curtained chamber.
sentences.s233-s30: Tahmatan checked the charge in mid‑rush; on that occasion he mounted Tiztag.
sentences.s233-s31: He commanded that they take provisions; then they set out upon the difficult road.
sentences.s233-s32: They took to the wilderness and the long road came before them; thus came a battle‑ready army.
sentences.s233-s49: Khosrow gazed at those companions — the wild beasts, the elephants, and those weary ones.
sentences.s233-s50: He drew in the reins and set out on the road; from his head he took up that Kayanian cap.
sentences.s233-s51: He descended and, before God, flung himself to the ground, rolled, and said, 'O pure sovereign!'
sentences.s233-s52: An oppressor has done injustice against me; he has made me fatherless, with pain and grief.
sentences.s233-s21: With the king and a thousand elephants and savage beasts, they lifted their baggage from that battlefield.
sentences.s233-s22: Fereyburz, son of Kavus, set off joyfully; he mustered troops near Khosrow and advanced.
sentences.s233-s23: Also went with him, so they say, the nobles of Piltan and the regiments of that assembly.
sentences.s233-s24: He took him aside to bid farewell; tears poured forth from the king's sorrow.
sentences.s233-s17: All the kings who have fetters — I took from the elephants a coiled snare.
sentences.s233-s18: I now have counsel and resolve for battle — unless someone comes before my mace to submit.
sentences.s233-s19: May tongues be full of praise of you; may the summit of the revolving sky and the earth be yours.
sentences.s233-s20: When the sealed letter arrived, Badad gave it to the chief Fariborz, scion of Khosrow.
sentences.s233-s37: That from India, Sқlab, Turan and China they will no longer recite it; henceforth, go — 'Afarin'.
sentences.s233-s38: He struck the war-drum; a cloud of dust rose from the plain. The air was full of dust and the ground full of men.
sentences.s233-s39: From among those renowned, quarrelsome men Babur stepped forward; a certain one spoke.
sentences.s233-s40: They went two stages from that place, for the world had grown dark because of the slain.
sentences.s233-s25: From that place he moved toward the army, and when the night appeared like two curled locks.
sentences.s233-s26: They sat in calm with the rūd (string instrument) and with wine; one hand (on) the rūd and the other hand (on) the ney.
sentences.s233-s27: They departed, each in his own calm; each took away his own satisfaction.
sentences.s233-s28: When the sun, in the hue of yellow brocade, oppressed the mass of lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s233-s41: They spotted a grove and someone dismounted there; the whole plain and the river were blackened by the host.
sentences.s233-s42: There was tranquility and repose, with wine in hand; one was merry and joyful, another sleeping and intoxicated.
sentences.s233-s43: A messenger came from every land — from every notable and every chief.
sentences.s233-s44: They brought many gifts, musical instruments, and some offerings to that famed man.
sentences.s233-s45: When a few days had passed since that tale, the lofty heaven's wheel ceased its turning.
sentences.s233-s46: An army came before the King of Iran; it was that Farribarz, King Kāvus, had arrived.
sentences.s233-s47: The king received him — the attendants welcomed him with horns and drums and heavy troops.
sentences.s233-s48: Farībarz came close to Khosrow, kissed the ground, and saw who it was.
sentences.s233-s1: He summoned the worldly‑wise scribe before him and had him express whatever words needed to be said.
sentences.s233-s2: He commanded that the royal letter be written in Pahlavi with amber.
sentences.s233-s3: As a heading he wrote, “Praise be to God — wherever He is, may He always remain in His place.”
sentences.s233-s4: Worthy (of praise) are the Moon, Kayvān and Hūr — the writer, inscriber of farr (royal glory), of the land and of might.
sentences.s233-s13: All the kings of the realm were undone — not swept away by the wind, but ruined by a lean fate.
sentences.s233-s14: Between two mountains, over ridge and plain, it was not possible to pass because of blood and the slain.
sentences.s233-s15: Indeed it measured forty parasangs; the earth was strewn with blood like a flower.
sentences.s233-s16: Finally, about this long-standing sovereignty: if I tell its tale, this record will become long.
